coredata.dc:description: Using amino acid ionic liquids (Ser–TEA) as surface modifiers and citric acid (CA) the carbon source, ionised carbon quantum dots (Ser–CA/CQDs) of uniform size were successfully prepared in this study. They exhibited excellent dispersion stability and tribological performance in an ethylene glycol aqueous solution (EGaq), as revealed using a one-step pyrolysis method. Macroscopic experimental results show that adding 2.0 wt % Ser–CA/CQDs reduces the friction coefficient of EGaq from 0.16 to 0.128, and the wear scar volume decreases by 51.97 %, which is attributed to the formation of a hydration layer and tribofilm. Furthermore, molecular dynamics simulations at the microscopic level revealed that the weak interaction energy between Ser–CA/CQDs and the friction pair surface, combined with the "ball-bearing effect", significantly reduced frictional resistance. Further, the lubrication performance of EGaq improved with the formation of a carbon film and an interface with low shear strength. This process offers a novel atomic-level perspective for understanding and optimising the applications of nanolubricant additives.

head.ce:title: Macroscopic tribological properties and molecular simulation study of carbon quantum dots modified by ionic liquids

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Using amino acid ionic liquids (Ser–TEA) as surface modifiers and citric acid (CA) the carbon source, ionised carbon quantum dots (Ser–CA/CQDs) of uniform size were successfully prepared in this study. They exhibited excellent dispersion stability and tribological performance in an ethylene glycol aqueous solution (EGaq), as revealed using a one-step pyrolysis method. Macroscopic experimental results show that adding 2.0 wt % Ser–CA/CQDs reduces the friction coefficient of EGaq from 0.16 to 0.128, and the wear scar volume decreases by 51.97 %, which is attributed to the formation of a hydration layer and tribofilm. Furthermore, molecular dynamics simulations at the microscopic level revealed that the weak interaction energy between Ser–CA/CQDs and the friction pair surface, combined with the "ball-bearing effect", significantly reduced frictional resistance. Further, the lubrication performance of EGaq improved with the formation of a carbon film and an interface with low shear strength. This process offers a novel atomic-level perspective for understanding and optimising the applications of nanolubricant additives.

ce:section.ce:para: Intense friction and wear are often observed in the field of mechanical engineering, particularly in machining processes. Insufficient cooling and lubrication in the machining area cause an increase in heat flux density and cutting forces, inevitably leading to tool failure, plastic deformation of the workpiece, and severe wear on the machined surface [1]. Furthermore, intense friction can severely affect the manufacturing accuracy and production efficiency, causing significant energy and economic losses. The use of an appropriate lubrication technology can effectively reduce friction and minimise wear on material surfaces [2]. With the growing awareness of environmental challenges and the continuous advancement of machining technology, traditional mineral oil-based lubricants are increasingly being criticised for containing environmentally harmful elements, such as sulfur and phosphorus. In recent years, water-based lubricants have become promising candidates for adoption as high-performance lubricants owing to their wide availability, eco-friendliness, and excellent cooling properties [3,4]. However, owing to their inherently low viscosity, water-based lubricants struggle to form long-lasting and stable lubrication films at the interface of the friction pair. Hence, the development of efficient and stable water-soluble lubricant additives is an effective approach to enhance the overall performance of water-based lubricants and broaden their applications on the surfaces of friction pairs, thereby satisfying the long-term service requirements [5,6].

In recent years, nanoadditives have attracted attention owing to their ability to easily penetrate the friction interface, as well as their excellent extreme-pressure and anti-wear properties. Consequently, many researchers have focused on investigating their tribological performance as lubricant additives. Common nanomaterial additives include metallic elements, SiO2, and carbon-based nanomaterials. Unfortunately, these nanomaterials have poor long-term dispersion stability in base lubricants and are prone to sedimentation and agglomeration. These issues severely restrict the industrialisation and large-scale application of nanomaterial additives [7–10]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are 0D nanocarbon materials discovered in the early 2000s. They have attracted attention because of their flexible structural design, environmental friendliness, abundant raw material sources, and the presence of hydrophilic functional groups, such as –OH and –COOH, on their surfaces [11–13]. Since 2015, CQDs have been introduced as nanolubricant additives and numerous researchers have focused on studying their tribological performance as water-soluble additives [14–16]. Jin et al. [17] used benzopyrrole as the raw material to synthesise carbon dots (N, P-CDs) via a hydrothermal method. The results showed that when N, P-CDs were used as aqueous-soluble additives, they exhibited excellent lubrication performance. Compared to pure water, the friction coefficient (COF) decreased by 67.9 % and the wear volume (WV) by 39.8 %. In addition, the synthesised N, P-CDs exhibited notable antibacterial properties. Tang et al. [18] developed polyethylene glycol (PEG)-functionalized carbon nitride quantum dots (CNQDs–PEG) with a uniform size and excellent crystallinity. When CNQDs–PEG were used as a nanolubricant additive in PEG 200, they exhibited excellent tribological performance with the addition of only 0.35 wt %. This significantly improved the tribological properties of the base lubricant. Chimeno-Trinchet et al. [19] used ionic liquids (ILs) and glutathione as carbon sources and modified carbon dots with IL cations. The resulting nanomaterials had diameters ranging from 2.2 to 3.5 nm and exhibited long-term stability when used as nanolubricant additives. When carbon dots prepared with ILs were used as nanolubricant additives, they exhibited optimal lubrication properties with only 0.1 % addition. Under extreme loads, the COF decreased by 30 %, and the WV decreased by more than 60 %.

Numerous researchers in related fields have reported the use of CQDs as nanolubricant additives [20–23]. Generally, the lubrication mechanisms of nanoparticles used as additives can be summarised in two ways. First, during the friction process, nanoparticles quickly penetrate the friction interface and undergo tribochemical reactions under external stress, forming a tribofilm. Second, the CQDs provide lubrication to the metal friction pair surface through mechanisms such as rolling, self-repair, and polishing [24–27]. Although numerous studies have been conducted on the macroscopic tribological performance of CQDs as nanolubricant additives, advanced microscopic characterisation techniques have attributed the lubrication mechanism of CQDs primarily to the formation of a tribofilm on the metal surface. However, at the atomic scale, lubricant molecules and nanoparticles exhibit special scale effects that differ from those of macroscopic lubrication. In this study, a simple one-step pyrolysis method was used to prepare IL-modified CQDs, which were used as water-soluble nanolubricant additives. Their friction-reducing and antiwear properties were evaluated via macroscopic tribological tests. By combining molecular dynamics (MD) simulations, the microscopic dynamic behavior of nanoparticles on metal surfaces was examined at the atomic scale, providing a more detailed understanding of adsorption kinetics to complement macroscopic tribological experiments. This approach also offers deeper insight into the lubrication mechanism of nanoparticles.

Serine (C3H7NO3, 99 %) was purchased from the Beijing Beilingwei Technology Co., Ltd., triethanolamine (C6H15NO3, 98 %) was purchased from the Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd., and ethylene glycol ((CH2OH)2, AR, 98 %) was supplied by the Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. All the materials were used as received. The reagents used in CQD preparation, including anhydrous ethanol and deionised water, were of analytical grade.

The ILs used to modify the CQDs were prepared via a proton exchange reaction between amino acids and triethanolamine (Fig. S1) [28,29]. Specifically, 5 g of serine and 7.17 g of triethanolamine were placed in a round-bottom flask with anhydrous ethanol and reacted at 70 °C for 24 h. After completion of the reaction, the solvent and excess water were removed via vacuum evaporation. The resulting product was then placed in a drying oven at 80 °C overnight to yield the amino alcohol ILs, designated as Ser–TEA. Amino alcohol IL-modified CQDs were synthesised via a one-step pyrolysis method using citric acid (CA) as the carbon source. Specifically, 3 g of CA and 4.41 g of Ser–TEA were placed in a flask and carbonised in an oil bath at 210 °C for 4 h. After cooling to room temperature, deionised water was added to fully dissolve the product. The solution was then centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 30 min, and the supernatant was collected and placed in a 1000 Da dialysis bag. Dialysis was performed in deionised water for 3 days, with the water being replaced frequently. The product inside the dialysis bag was vacuum evaporated to eliminate excess water. Finally, the product was freeze-dried for 24 h and then dried in an oven at 80 °C for another 24 h to obtain a brown powder, designated as Ser–CA/CQDs (Fig. S1).

A green, nontoxic aqueous ethylene glycol solution (EGaq, with a mass ratio of deionised water to ethylene glycol of 1:1) was chosen as the base lubricant. EGaq was prepared by thoroughly mixing the components using a magnetic stirrer for 24 h. The prepared water-soluble Ser–TEA and Ser–CA/CQDs were added to EGaq at various additive concentrations (0.3–2.0 wt %). After thorough dispersion, Ser–TEA and Ser–CA/CQDs–EGaq dispersions of different concentrations were obtained (hereinafter referred to as (Ser–TEA)EGaq and (Ser–CA/CQDs)EGaq, respectively).

Digital images of each dispersion were captured using a camera immediately after preparation and after 30 d of settling. Fig. S2(a) and (b) show digital images of (Ser–CA/CQDs)EGaq at various concentrations on the first day and after 30 d, respectively. As shown in the images, no significant aggregation or sedimentation was observed in the dispersions after 30 d, indicating that the prepared Ser–CA/CQDs possessed long-term and stable dispersibility in water-based lubricants.

The microstructure of Ser–CA/CQDs was observed via transmission electron microscopy (TEM; FEI TECNAI G2 F20 200 Kv, USA). The phase composition of Ser–CA/CQDs was analysed via X-ray diffraction (XRD; BRUKER, D8 ADVANCE). The elemental composition of the Ser–CA/CQDs was characterised and analysed via Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR; ALPHA II, BRUKER) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi). The dispersion stabilities of the samples in water were measured using a Shimadzu UV-2550 UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The luminescence behaviour of the Ser–CA/CQDs aqueous dispersion was analysed using a HORIBA Aqualog fluorescence spectrometer (Japan).

To evaluate the macroscopic tribological properties of the IL-modified CQDs, the friction-reducing performances of EGaq, (Ser–TEA)EGaq, and (Ser–CA/CQDs)EGaq at the steel interface were tested using a reciprocating micro-tribometer (SRV–V, Optimol, Germany). The upper (Φ = 10 mm) and lower (Φ = 24 mm, d = 7.9 mm) friction pairs were both made of AISI 52100 bearing steel. Both had a Rockwell hardness of 58–62 HRC and a surface roughness of ≤0.05 μm. The tribological testing conditions were set with a load of 100–300 N, a micro-motion frequency of 25 Hz, a stroke of 1 mm, and a test duration of 0.5 h. The surfaces of the upper and lower friction pairs were cleaned with anhydrous ethanol before and after testing.

After the tribological tests, the WV of the worn scars on the surface of the steel disk were measured using a 3D optical profiler (BRUKER-NPFLEX) to evaluate the antiwear performance of each dispersant. Subsequently, the surface features of the wear scars were characterised via scanning electron microscopy (SEM) using a Thermo Scientific Apreo 2 instrument. The elemental and bonding states of the wear scar surfaces were analysed via XPS (NEXSA, Thermo Scientific).
The details of the molecular dynamics simulations can be found in the Supporting Information.

The microstructures of Ser–CA/CQDs were observed via TEM. Fig. 1 (a) and (b) show the structure and TEM images of Ser–CA/CQDs, respectively. The TEM images clearly show that Ser–CA/CQDs possess a quasi-spherical structure. The electrostatic repulsion between the IL groups effectively prevented the aggregation of Ser–CA/CQDs in the dispersion medium [26]. Therefore, the prepared Ser–CA/CQDs exhibited excellent dispersibility in water. Owing to their low crystallinity, no distinct lattice fringes were observed in the Ser–CA/CQDs. The particle size distribution of the Ser–CA/CQDs showed that their sizes were less than 10 nm, with an average particle size of 3.34 nm. According to the XRD pattern (Fig. 1(c)), a broad characteristic peak appears at approximately 2θ = 23.8°, indicating that Ser–CA/CQDs are primarily composed of amorphous carbon [30]. Fig. 1(d) displays the FT-IR spectrum of Ser–CA/CQDs. The characteristic absorption peak near 3412 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of the O–H and amide N–H bonds. The absorption peak at 2308 cm−1 corresponds to NH+, while the peaks at 1703, 1594, and 1070 cm−1 are attributed to the C═O, –COO–, and C–O bonds, respectively [31]. This suggests that the surface of Ser–CA/CQDs was modified with numerous oxygen functional groups. The peak at 1348 cm−1 corresponds to the C–N stretching vibrations in the Ser–CA/CQDs. The absorption peaks at approximately 1348 cm−1 and 862–425 cm−1 correspond to the bending vibrations of –CH– and the bending vibrations of –CH in –CH2–CH2–, respectively [32].

Fig. 2 shows the full-scan XPS spectrum of Ser–CA/CQDs (a) and the high-resolution spectra of the individual elements (b–d). The full XPS spectrum revealed that the Ser–CA/CQDs primarily consisted of carbon, oxygen, and nitrogens. Interestingly, the higher oxygen content primarily originated from the abundant oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of the Ser–CA/CQDs. The C1s spectrum can be deconvoluted into three peaks at binding energies of 287.8, 285.7, and 284.5 eV, attributed to N–C═O/O–C═O, C–N/C–O, and C═C/C–C bonds in Ser–CA/CQDs, respectively [33–35]. In the high-resolution O1s spectrum, fitted peaks corresponding to C═O (531.6 eV), C–O (532.6 eV), and O–H bonds (530.4 eV) can be clearly observed [36–38]. In addition, the high-resolution N1s spectrum exhibits broad absorption peaks at approximately 401.1 and 399.4 eV, which were attributed to graphite nitrogen and organic amine salts, respectively [39–41]. This analysis further confirmed that the surface of Ser–CA/CQDs was modified with hydroxyl, carboxyl, and carbonyl groups. Based on these results, we performed an ionisation modification of the CA/CQDs.

Furthermore, we investigated the optical properties of Ser–CA/CQD aqueous dispersions. Fig. 3 (a) shows the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the Ser–CA/CQDs dispersion in water. As reported in a previous study [42,43], the light absorption detected at approximately 250 nm and 350 nm is attributed to the π–π* transitions in the C═C bonds and the n–π* transitions in the C═O/C═N bonds. The figures show the Ser–CA/CQD aqueous dispersion under sunlight and UV-light (λ = 365 nm). As shown in the figures, the Ser–CA/CQD aqueous dispersion appears orange–yellow under sunlight and exhibits intense blue fluorescence under UV-light. This is a distinctive feature of the CQDs, which further confirms the successful preparation of Ser–CA/CQDs. The photoluminescence (PL) spectrum of the Ser–CA/CQD aqueous dispersion (Fig. 3(b)) confirms that its photoluminescent behaviour is independent of the excitation wavelength. This luminescence mechanism is attributed to a multiphoton process [44,45]. As the excitation wavelength increased from 360 to 390 nm, the maximum emission peak shifted from 463 to 483 nm, highlighting a significant excitation-dependent emission behaviour of the prepared Ser–CA/CQDs. This is primarily attributed to the complex surface states of the Ser–CA/CQDs. The above analysis suggests that the Ser–CA/CQDs demonstrate excellent dispersibility in water and exhibit photoluminescent behaviour.

Considering that base lubricants are particularly sensitive to the dosage of additive molecules or nano-additives, we first evaluated the effect of the dosage of Ser–TEA and Ser–CA/CQDs on the tribological performance of the corresponding dispersions. Fig. 4 (a) and (b) show the COF curves and corresponding average COF at a 300 N load for EGaq, as well as the different concentrations of (Ser–TEA)EGaq and (Ser–CA/CQDs)EGaq. The results of room-temperature tribological tests showed that the average COF of EGaq was approximately 0.16. When Ser–TEA or Ser–CA/CQDs were introduced, the COF of the corresponding dispersions decreased, and the COF further decreased with increasing additive concentration. The average COF values for 2 % Ser–TEA and 2 % Ser–CA/CQDs were 0.130 and 0.128, respectively. Compared with EGaq, the average COF values of 2 % Ser–TEA and 2 % Ser–CA/CQDs were reduced by 18.75 % and 20 %, respectively. As indicated by this phenomenon, the Ser–CA/CQDs exhibited excellent friction-reducing performance under heavy loads because of the abundance of hydrophilic groups on their surfaces, resulting in a stronger hydration capacity [63]. Therefore, we selected 2 % as the optimal concentration of Ser–TEA and Ser–CA/CQDs for this study.

The tribological performance of EGaq, 2 % Ser-TEA, and 2 % Ser-CA/CQDs under different loads at room temperature was evaluated. Fig. 5 (a–c) shows the COF curves and corresponding WV for each dispersion under loads of 100–300 N (Fig. 5(a1–c1)). The test results indicate that EGaq experienced adhesive wear early in the friction test owing to the difficulty experienced by the base lubricant molecules in forming a strong and stable lubrication film on the surface of the tribological pair. Notably, under the same load, the average COF and WV of 2 % Ser–CA/CQDs were lower than those of the other two dispersion systems. When the load was 100 N, the average COF of the 2 % Ser–CA/CQDs was 18.75 % lower than that of EGaq, and the corresponding WV was reduced by 47.53 %. As the load increased further, the corresponding WV of each dispersion increased, whereas the fluctuation amplitude of the COF curve decreased. When the load was 300 N, adhesive wear occurred at approximately 100 s for the 2 % Ser–CA/CQDs, indicating that the CQDs may agglomerate at the tribological interface under heavy-load conditions, leading to a sudden increase in the COF. However, considering the overall average COF, the 2 % Ser–CA/CQDs still demonstrated a superior lubrication effect under harsh conditions, with their WV reduced by 51.97 % compared with that of EGaq. Therefore, increasing the load contributed to the stabilisation of the corresponding COF curves of Ser–TEA, causing the IL dispersion systems to exhibit a more stable friction-reducing performance. However, this is detrimental to the uniform dispersion of CQDs. In addition, heavy load conditions further exacerbated the wear of the tribological surface. The test results shown in Fig. 5 indicate that under lower load conditions, 2 % Ser–CA/CQDs contribute to better lubrication performance on the surface of engineering steel.

Table 1 compares the tribological performance of the ionised CQDs prepared in this study as nanoadditives with those of reported in similar studies in the past. Owing to the differences in the friction environment, the tribological performances of various types of CQDs as nanoadditives show certain differences. When neglecting factors such as frequency, stroke, and time during the friction process, and focusing only on the applied load, the ionised CQDs prepared in this study still exhibit a low COF (0.128) and WV (4.1 × 104 μm3) under a heavy load (300 N). Therefore, their friction-reducing and anti-wear performances were superior to those reported in most previous studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface analysis of the wear scars
ce:section.ce:para: SEM was used to visualise the surface morphology of the wear scars for each dispersion under a 100 N load. Fig. 6 (a–c) shows the SEM images of the wear scars corresponding to EGaq, 2 % Ser–TEA, and 2 % Ser–CA/CQDs, respectively. After lubrication with EGaq, the wear scar exhibited a wider and rougher surface morphology. As shown in Fig. 6(a1), the surface displays severe scratches and deeper grooves. In addition, multiple large and deep pits caused by contact fatigue were observed, which resulted from the EGaq molecules failing to form a long-lasting and stable protective film on the metal surface. With the introduction of Ser–TEA, the wear scar for 2 % Ser–TEA (Fig. 6(b)) became narrower, with a significant reduction in the number of scratches. The grooves were also considerably less severe than those in the EGaq. At this point, only a few small, shallow pits were observed. This indicates that the introduction of Ser–TEA, to some extent, improved the lubrication performance of the corresponding dispersion on the surface of engineering steel. This phenomenon is attributed to the strong boundary film-forming ability of IL molecules, where the active elements undergo tribochemical reactions under shear stress and frictional heat, forming a strong tribofilm on the metal surface. In contrast, when the nanoadditive Ser–CA/CQDs were introduced, both the length and width of the wear scar (Fig. 6(c)) were became than those in the previous two systems, with fewer scratches on the worn surface. Smooth areas are clearly visible in certain regions. The nanoadditive Ser–CA/CQDs can be inferred to have polishing and self-healing effects, which significantly enhanced the tribological performance of EGaq.

To further investigate the potential tribochemical reactions that occurred during lubrication with 2 % Ser–CA/CQDs, XPS analysis was performed, revealing the elements and bonding states on the corresponding wear scar surface. Fig. 7 (a–d) shows the high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s, O1s, N1s, and Fe2p, respectively. The absorption peak at 284.4 eV in the C1s high-resolution spectrum corresponds to the standard peak position, which results from the formation of C–C and C═C bonds [46,47]. A broad absorption peak at approximately 288.2 eV suggests the formation of C═O or N–C═O bonds on the wear scar surface [48,49]. The absorption peak at 286.1 eV in the C1s spectrum, along with the absorptions at 532.6 eV in the O1s and 401.2 eV in the N1s spectra, indicates the presence of C–O and C–N bonds on the wear scar surface. The absorption peak near 530.6 eV in the O1s spectrum is due to the formation of metal oxides on the friction surface. The absorption peak fitted at 399.3 eV in the N1s spectrum corresponds to N–H bond [50–52]. In addition, the Fe2p spectrum was deconvoluted into multiple absorption peaks, suggesting the presence of various iron oxides on the wear scar surface, including FeO, Fe2O3, Fe3O4, and FeO(OH) [53–55,68]. These results provide strong evidence that Ser–CA/CQDs participated in tribochemical reactions, resulting in the formation of tribochemical products containing carbon, nitrogen, and iron oxides. These products then form a tribofilm, which significantly improves macroscopic lubrication at the metal contact interface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microscopic dynamic behaviour at the interface
ce:section.ce:para: Previous studies have shown that CQDs tend to adsorb onto friction surfaces through electrostatic forces, forming a carbon film that improves friction and wear performance by isolating direct contact between the friction surfaces [56]. However, on the atomic scale, CQDs exhibit unique scale effects that differ from those of macroscopic lubrication. To further explore the potential lubrication mechanism of Ser–CA/CQDs at the engineered steel interface, we employed MD to investigate the dynamic adsorption processes of EGaq, (Ser–TEA)EGaq, and (Ser–CA/CQD)EGaq during the friction process at the atomic level. As shown in Fig. S5, snapshots of the three shear models were captured at 0, 100, 300, and 500 ps? After lubrication with EGaq, no significant adsorption of lubricant molecules was observed on the Fe (110) surface. A snapshot of the (Ser–TEA)EGaq system clearly shows molecular aggregation on the Fe (110) surface, with Ser–TEA molecules adsorbed nearly flat onto the metal surface (Movie S1, Supporting Information). A snapshot of the (Ser–CA/CQD)EGaq system shows that the MD of Ser–CA/CQDs at the fluid interface is primarily dominated by rolling motion. Additionally, because the structure of CQDs is similar to that of multilayer graphene, during the shear process, some Ser–CA/CQDs were adsorbed onto the Fe (110) surface under electrostatic forces, forming a carbon film on the friction surface (Movie S2, Supporting Information).

To better clarify this effect, we calculated the E interaction between the molecules and the Fe (110) interface for the three systems, as shown in Fig. 8 . The calculation results show that the E interaction between the lubricants and the Fe (110) interface is mainly attributed to electrostatic and van der Waals forces, with electrostatic energy playing a dominant role in adsorption. After the introduction of Ser–TEA, the contribution of the van der Waals forces to the adsorption energy was reduced. At this point, 73.32 % of the E adsorption of the additive molecules on the Fe (110) surface was attributed to electrostatic forces. Notably, after the introduction of Ser–CA/CQDs, the contribution of van der Waals forces to the adsorption energy was further reduced. At this point, the contribution of the electrostatic energy was 83.37 %. Compared to the other two systems, the E interaction between Ser–CA/CQDs and the Fe (110) surface was the lowest. In contrast, Ser–CA/CQDs exhibited a lower relative frictional resistance at the metal interface, providing a more shearable interface for tribological contact.

Fig. S6 shows the real-time simulated COF and corresponding average COF bar charts for the three friction systems during the shear simulation. The results show that the simulated COF curves for EGaq, (Ser–TEA)EGaq, and (Ser–CA/CQDs)EGaq exhibited trends similar to those observed in the macroscopic tribological experiments. With the introduction of Ser–TEA and Ser–CA/CQDs, the average simulated COF values for the corresponding dispersions decreased by 36.3 % and 42.26 %, respectively, suggesting that both additives effectively enhanced the friction and wear performance of The results suggest that a higher E interaction between the lubricants in EGaq and the Fe (110) interface contributes to a greater resistance to the relative motion at the interface, which in turn leads to higher simulated real-time COF values. In contrast, the very weak E interaction between Ser–CA/CQDs and the Fe (110) interface, combined with the "ball-bearing effect" of the nanoparticles, creates an interface with low shear strength in the tribological system. Additionally, the layered structure of the Ser–CA/CQDs underwent strong adsorption on the metal surface owing to the dominant electrostatic and secondary van der Waals forces, leading to the formation of a carbon film. The rolling effect of the nanoparticles, combined with the adsorption of the carbon film significantly reduces the frictional resistance during the relative sliding process at the metal interface, thereby leading to a lower COF value during the shear process.

Previous studies reported that friction-induced wear causes the release of low-energy electrons from the asperities of metal surfaces in actual contact, leading to the accumulation of positively charged points on the surfaces [57,58]. Accordingly, a shearing environment with positively charged Fe (110) surfaces was established in the friction system under an applied load, and shear simulations were conducted for the corresponding configurations. The dynamic adsorption processes of the ILs or CQDs on the metal interface were quantified by tracking the motion trajectories of the anions in Ser–TEA and the centre of mass of the Ser–CA/CQDs within the blended systems. Fig. S7(a) illustrates the real-time trajectory of the Ser–TEA anions under an applied load in the <0 0–1> direction within the friction system. Fig. S7(b) illustrates the real-time motion trajectory of the Ser–TEA anions in the friction system when a load was applied in the <0 0–1> direction and an electric field is applied in the <0, 0, 1> direction. The analysis shows that after the Fe (110) surface releases emitted electrons, the anions in the Ser–TEA tend to accumulate in the <0 0–1> direction, with the majority concentrated within 20 Å of the Fe (110) surface (Fig. S7(b)). The emitted electrons and Ser–TEA anions form an adsorptive protective film under the synergistic effects of electrostatic and van der Waals forces. The closer this film is to the friction pair surface, the more difficult it is to detach in during shear, which facilitates the formation of at tribochemical reaction film.

Similarly, we tracked the centre-of-mass motion trajectory of the Ser–CA/CQDs in the blended system. Fig. S8(a) shows the real-time motion trajectory of the Ser–CA/CQDs when a load was applied along the <0 0–1> direction in the friction system. Fig. S8(b) shows the real-time motion trajectory of the Ser–CA/CQDs when a load was applied in the <0 0–1> direction and an electric field was applied in the <0 0 1> direction in the friction system. As shown in Fig. S8(a), during the shear process, the motion trajectory of the Ser–CA/CQDs was more scattered when a load was applied only to the friction system. When low-energy electrons are released from the Fe (110) surface, the motion trajectory of Ser–CA/CQDs becomes more compact, with a concentration mainly at approximately 33.2 Å from the Fe (110) surface. A possible explanation is that under mechanical stress or an applied electric field, certain particles of Ser–CA/CQDs near the surface of the friction pair may resonate or undergo dissociative trapping owing to their unique layered structure. These particles are adsorbed onto the metal surface via electrostatic and van der Waals forces to form a carbon film. This film reduces the strong E interaction between the base lubricants and Fe (110), thereby lowering the relative motion resistance at the shear interface. Some Ser–CA/CQDs particles are located slightly farther from the Fe (110) substrate and contribute to the rolling effect. Combined with the strong adsorption between the carbon film and metal surface, this reduces the frictional resistance at the sliding interface, thereby improving the lubrication performance of the base lubricant on the engineering steel surface.

As suggested by the results of the macroscopic experiments and microscopic simulations, the friction reduction and anti-wear resistance mechanism of Ser–CA/CQDs as a nanoadditive can be attributed to the synergistic effects of multiple factors. As shown in Fig. 9 , during the initial stage of friction, owing to their small size, spherical structure, and excellent dispersion stability, Ser–CA/CQDs can continuously, stably, and rapidly penetrate the contact region of the friction pairs, thereby filling pits and microcracks on the metal surface and exerting repair and physical isolation effects of the CQDs [59,60]. Furthermore, the microasperities on the metal surface released low-energy electrons, causing the friction pair surface to become positively charged. Owing to their unique layered structure, some Ser–CA/CQDs captured these electrons through resonance or dissociation and were adsorbed onto the metal surface through the synergistic effects of electrostatic and van der Waals forces. The formed adsorption film can effectively shield the strong interaction energy between the base lubricant molecules and the metal surface, reducing the relative motion resistance at the sliding interface while providing nitrogen as an active element for tribochemical reactions [61]. Subsequently, the active elements on the surface of the Ser–CA/CQDs participated in tribochemical reactions under the combined influence of external mechanical stress and frictional heat stress, forming a tribofilm composed of carbon, nitrogen, and iron oxides, which effectively isolated the direct contact between the friction pair surfaces. Meanwhile, the hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of Ser–CA/CQDs formed an extensive hydrogen bond network with the water molecules, creating a stable hydration layer on the tribofilm. Additionally, some Ser–CA/CQDs, which were farther away from the friction pair surface, contributed to further reduction in the frictional resistance at the sliding interface through the rolling ball effect. This phenomenon occurred in collaboration with the strong adsorption interaction between the tribofilm and metal surface, thereby improving the lubrication performance of the base lubricant on the engineering steel surface [62,63].

In this study, we successfully synthesised Ser–CA/CQDs via a simple one-step pyrolysis method, using CA as the carbon source and amino alcohol ILs as the modifier. Owing to their minimal and uniform size (approximately 2.201 ± 0.86 nm), Ser–CA/CQDs exhibited long-lasting and stable dispersion performance as a water-soluble nanolubricant additive and demonstrated excellent friction-reducing and anti-wear properties. Macroscopic tribological studies indicated that incorporating 2.0 wt % Ser–CA/CQDs into EGaq reduced the COF and WV of the base lubricant by 20.0 % and 51.97 %, respectively. The exceptional tribological performance of Ser–CA/CQDs can be primarily attributed to their surface being enriched with hydrophilic functional groups and the tribologically active nitrogen. This composition enhances their hydration capability while also facilitating the repair and filling of pores on the surface of the metal friction pairs. Tribologically active nitrogen enhances the involvement of CQDs in tribochemical reactions, resulting in the formation of a tribofilm containing carbon, nitrogen, and iron oxides. As indicated by the microscopic MD simulation results, the adsorption of Ser–CA/CQDs in the fluid region shields the strong interaction energy between the lubricant molecules and the Fe (110) interface, thereby providing a lower resistance to relative motion at the shear interface. Additionally, the rolling effect further reduces frictional resistance during the interface sliding process.
